summary: Joel Spolsky briefly promotes his article on resume writing, which advises job seekers to avoid generic, book-copied cover letters. He shares the polarized feedback the article received—some readers found it invaluable career advice while others found it pompous. The post illustrates how direct, opinionated career advice tends to split audiences between those who appreciate bluntness and those who find it condescending.
key_points: Generic cover letters copied from books make candidates look like 'bullshit artists' who can't form original thoughts
Practical career advice tends to generate sharply polarized reactions—people either love it or hate it
The disparity in feedback likely reflects whether readers saw themselves in the criticism or not
money_quotes: If you write 'I understand the position also requires a candidate who is team- and detail-oriented, works well under pressure, and is able to deal with people in departments throughout the firm' then at best people will think you're a bullshit artist and at worst they will think that you were not born with the part of the brain that allows you to form your own thoughts and ideas.
key_insight: Generic cover letter language signals an inability to think independently, which is the opposite of what hiring managers want to see.
